Title: Identification and Characterization of CtUGT3 as the Key Player of Astragalin Biosynthesis in Carthamus tinctorius L.
Journal: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Kaempferol; also kaempferol 7-O-glucoside; naringenin; naringenin chalcone; apigenin; quercetin; isorhamnetin
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Astragalin (kaempferol 3-O-glucoside); kaempferol 7-O-glucoside; kaempferol 3,7-di-O-glucoside; other flavonoid O-glucosides
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to flavonoid hydroxyl groups, with validated 3-OH glycosylation of kaempferol to form astragalin and additional 7-OH glycosylation activity in vitro.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for astragalin formation supported by in vivo overexpression and enzymology
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes astragalin accumulation; astragalin content more than doubled upon overexpression in safflower corolla protoplasts
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Metabolomics-transcriptomics correlation; transient overexpression in safflower corolla protoplasts; metabolite profiling by UPLC-QTOF-MS/MS; recombinant protein enzyme assays in vitro; kinetic analysis; molecular docking; site-directed mutagenesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CtUGT3 was identified from integrated metabolome-transcriptome analysis as a UGT candidate associated with flavonoid O-glycosides. 2. Transient expression in safflower corolla protoplasts significantly increased astragalin, demonstrating in vivo involvement in astragalin biosynthesis. 3. Recombinant CtUGT3 glycosylated multiple flavonoids in vitro and showed both 3-OH and 7-OH glycosylation activities. 4. Kaempferol was catalytically most efficient among tested aglycones based on kcat/Km. 5. G15, T136, S276, and E384 were critical residues affecting glycosylation activity and regiospecificity.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expression increased under blue light and decreased under far-red light and dark conditions in safflower flowers
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expression increased under blue light and decreased under far-red light and dark conditions in safflower flowers
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Different light-quality treatments: blue, far-red, red, UV-B, white light, and dark
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for metabolic engineering to increase astragalin/flavonol O-glycosides in safflower flowers; catalytic residues may support enzyme engineering for improved 3-O glycosylation specificity.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although CtUGT3 showed both 3-OH and 7-OH glycosylation in vitro, only astragalin increased significantly in vivo in corolla protoplasts, indicating context-dependent product outcome and possible competition/cooperation with other enzymes.
